speaker: interviewer
text: Today is January 2, 1997. The survivor being interviewed is Janet Singer Applefield. The interviewer is Beth Cohen. The interview is being conducted in Plymouth, Massachusetts, United States of America. The interview is being conducted in English. Could you please state and spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's Janet Applefield, J-A-N-E-T A-P-P-L-E-F-I-E-L-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth? And could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gustawa Singer, G-U-S-T-A-W-A, Singer, S-I-N-G-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My nickname was Dzidzia, D-Z-I-D-Z-I-A. Just it means baby. And I had other names during the war. My name was Krystina Antoszkiewicz, K-R-Y-S-T-I-N-A A-N-T-O-S-Z-K-I-E-W-I-C-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other names? No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what's your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: June 4th, 1935.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and you're how old?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born? Please spell that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Krakow, Poland, C-R-A-C-OW, Poland, P-O-L-A-N-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks. Janet, why don't you begin by talking about your early memories of your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I lived in a little town, Nowy Targ, outside of Krakow, with a large extended family. I had a baby sister. And I was the first grandchild in the family. And my father was one of six brothers. And my mother was one of three sisters. And one set of grandparents, the Singers, lived in Nowy Targ. My other grandparents lived in Wadowice, a little village-- same place where the current Pope is from. And I remember a lot of fun things. I remember my uncles doing a lot of fun things with me in particular. My uncle Arthur used to take me out on his motorcycle, and we would go to the candy store, and he would buy me things. I remember preparation for the holidays when my mother and my grandmother used to cook and bake. I remember making challah, braiding challah with them, and making noodles. Everything was made from scratch, so it took a long time to prepare. And I remember the house being cleaned and-- but in particular, I recall the gypsy woman used to come. And she used to scrub-- you know, scrub the floors and get the whole house ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: For the holidays? Was this for--

speaker: interviewee
text: For the holi-- like for Friday night, for every Friday night when, you know, we'd have our Shabbos supper.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have for Shabbos supper?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had chicken soup, of course-- chicken, fish, cold fish. I think it was carp. And it was cooked with raisins. And I remember the gelatin from the fish. I remember certain dishes that to this day, I kind of favor, you know, like sliced cumbers called mizeria with cream and [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] borscht. You know, I mean, different things, but not always the same things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your grand-- you said your mother and your grandmother cooked together. Did your grandmother live with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, we lived in a-- I think it was an apartment. I don't remember exactly where we lived, but I-- it wasn't a house. But I remember a couple incidents that happened. Once-- well, I must have been two, three years old. There was a beautiful table cloth on the table, in the dining room table. My mother did a craft called tatting. It was kind of an embroidery, but lace-- sort of lace making. And I remember having a wonderful time cutting it up with scissors. And another time, I remember the housekeeper was cleaning out the bird cage. And the bird was on the balcony. And she told me to hold the door closed, you know, not to let him out. Of course, I did let him out and the neighbors running around looking for a canary that was flying around. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Different little things-- I remember the first time my parents went out. I was still in a crib. The crib was made out of like, a netting, like a rope, rope netting. And my parents were going out for the evening and leaving me with the housekeeper. And I remember you know, carrying on, screaming. And that was my first experience with separation. And it's so incredible, because I remember the design of the nightgown that I was wearing. And I must have been really little, because I was still in a crib. So things like that, you know, those very early memories-- but mainly the family and the warmth of the family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Amalja Nabel-- her maiden name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did your parents meet?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was an arranged meeting. And the story goes that when my mother first saw my father, she locked herself in a room and would not come out, because my father was a red head. In those days, red heads were really stigmatized. And you know, she didn't want to come out. But I guess-- I don't know how or how she was convinced, but she did come out. [LAUGHS] But so it was an arranged meeting.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old-- how old was your mother when she got married? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: She got married in 1934. And she was born in 1910. So it would make her 24.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she from Nowy Targ?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was from Wadowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a village-- not far. You know, we used to go there by train. So it wasn't very far.

speaker: interviewer
text: So her family lived there?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father's family lived--

speaker: interviewee
text: Lived in Nowy Targ.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- you mentioned Shabbos and holidays. Was your family religious when you were little?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather Nabel was very religious. And my other grandparents-- I think they practiced it, but I don't think they were-- I don't think they were very-- I think my father sort of put up a fuss, even though he did it. Because you know, there was either orthodoxy or there was nothing. So-- but I remember the men praying, you know, with the teffilin. But as I said, my grandfather, Jakob, was very religious. And we used to go there very often. I remember one time I was there, and I got very sick. I had scarlet fever. And so the treatment in those days is to be kept in a darkened room, and you had to-- you couldn't eat. You had to be on a strict diet. And I remember everybody would come in the room. And I would want to smell their breath to see what they ate. And to this day, I still smell tomato soup. And I love tomato soup. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: What about cousins? Were there cousins around?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were cousins. There was-- my father's brother was married. And they had a little girl, [? Anette, ?] Anushka. And then my mother's sister was married, and they had a little boy, [? Marzelic, ?] so I had little cousins.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your apartment look like? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just remember the balcony, where the bird-- I let the bird out. And I remember the dining room. And I remember my crib. And I remember one time stealing [LAUGHS] a little hot dog. They were all linked together, and I just pulled them and ate them raw-- things like that. But I don't remember the layout of the whole apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the neighborhood at all, what the area you lived in looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I remembered more because I've been there in recent years, and so I pictured that, but-- and also from the photographs that I have. Actually, the village has not changed at all. It's basically how it was, with the exception of the main square, which is called the [POLISH]. That used to be all cobblestone, and now it's a big parking lot, you know, that they use for parking. Oh, also I remember the market day. I think it was on a Thursday. And I particularly remember that, because that was the day when all the peasants-- the peasants were called [POLISH]. And they were-- came from the mountains. And they came to shop that day. And the market was right in the [POLISH]. Today it's not there anymore. It's a different location. But-- and that was a very good business day for my grandfather, who had the houseware, home improvement type of store.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Nowy Targ?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nowy Targ, right in the center of the town, in the [POLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father worked with my grandfather-- not willingly. He-- he had-- he wanted to become a dentist. And actually, he was-- the story goes that my father was on the train going to-- enrolled in a technical school to learn dentistry, and my grandfather pulled him off the train and made him stay. And being the oldest son, he had to do that. So my-- the other brothers sort of got away from-- [LAUGHS] wherever--

speaker: interviewer
text: How many other brothers were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was six altogether, including my father-- six brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work outside of the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she did not, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall if there were other Jews? I know your family lived there, but was there a Jewish community in Nowy Targ?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was. And I understand that pre-war, it was about 2,500. And there was a synagogue. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I do not.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the neighbors around where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I remember the-- the nurse maid that took care of me. Her name was [? Osza ?]. I remember her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she live in the house with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She lived in the house, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a baby sister. Her name was Sarenka. Well, it was Sarah, but we called her Sarenka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who-- who was older?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was older.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you were. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I was born in 1935. And she was born, I think, '38 or '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my sister died in 1940 or '41, after the war had began. She was sick with diphtheria. There were no medications. There was no-- there was no medical treatment at all. And she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any awareness of things changing in your community, or times changing, or of the war approaching, your parents talking about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember any changes before the war. After the war began, I became much more aware of something happen-- being very, very wrong.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get to that, I just want to ask you if there's anything else that you remember about your life in Nowy Targ? Was there anything special you liked to do? What did-- who did you play with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember some of my toys. I rem-- I don't remember playing with-- I actually don't remember playing with other children. But I remember the toys. And I remember the family. And I remember going out with my mother for walks. I remember the winters. I remember riding in a horse-drawn sled in the wintertime. That's how we really got around-- or even on skis. My father used to say he would keep a pair of skis at the door, so if he wanted to get around, he would use-- because there was so much snow all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about vacations, family vacations? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: The family vacations were mainly to visit my grandparents. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How often did you go to see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: It seemed like it was a lot. I don't know. I used to like to go there, because they had a big garden. They had a big house, and there was a garden behind their house. My grandfather used to make wine. And my grandmother used to can the fruits.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many children were there in your mother's family?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had three sisters. She was one of three.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the-- she was the middle daughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- did your aunts live close to your grandparents and close to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived-- one aunt lived with my grandparents. She was married, my oldest-- the oldest daughter. And the youngest daughter had gotten married, I guess after my mother and father got married. And they lived in a town called Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where--

speaker: interviewee
text: Also--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that near Nowy Targ?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also a train ride away, short train ride, all within maybe half hour or an hour, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was-- do you recall the nearest large city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Krakow was the large city nearby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever go there? Do you remember going there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember going there, but I think we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we had family there. My mother had family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you have gotten there? What was the--

speaker: interviewee
text: We would take the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else that you'd like to add about your extended family or your parents? What do you remember about your mother when you were a young child?

speaker: interviewee
text: I unfortunately remember so many other details, but I don't remember my mother very well. I just know some of the things that I've been told about her. I've been told that she was very bright, and she had a very good business sense-- something that I don't have. And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] but otherwise, I don't remember her.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened when the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: So some of the things I'm going to tell you is what I heard my father tell me, because I don't remember-- actually remember. But he said that he was awakened at 5 o'clock in the morning hearing shots, and shooting, or explosions, or-- and he turned on the radio. And he heard that Germany had invaded Poland. At that point, my father took me, and my mother, and my sister to my other grandparents' house. I'm not sure exactly at what point, whether he waited days, or probably you know, the next day or whatever. But he returned to Nowy Targ. And so I stayed with my grandparents, my mother, and my sister. And at that point, my grandfather loaded up the wagon-- horse and wagon. My grandfather was in the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] he traded hay and everything to do with horses. He had that kind of a shop. So he loaded up the wagon with the family-- my aunt and uncle-- two aunts and uncles, my mother and myself, my sister-- and as many belongings as we could fit into this wagon and two horses. And we joined a caravan of people heading eastward. And there were people walking, riding on horses or any which-- bicycles. And we were traveling for days and days and days, heading toward Russia. During this travel, I do remember a-- an airplane, a German plane. I think it was German. It may not have been a German, but an airplane that was shot down and fell out of the sky. We were being shot at. There were bullets. And we-- often, we had to get out of the wagon and hide in ditches and wait till the shooting stopped. Sometimes the roads were blocked. Sometimes bridges were out and we couldn't get across. And so we would have to go across the river. And so this continued until-- until we reached Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea why your father didn't join you on--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father returned to Nowy Targ. And he and his brothers and father joined the Polish army, if there was such a thing, and tried to repel the Germans. Apparently, the Polish army disbanded very shortly and they just came back. At that point, my father and his brothers [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] joined this caravan of people heading eastward. My grandmother and my grandfather and one uncle remained. They chose not to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father finally arrived in Russia, but he could-- he didn't know where we were. We were separated. He didn't know where we were. My father said-- so my father was in Lvov. We were in a town called [? Wutsk. ?] I don't think that was terribly far from-- the two towns were close by. And my father advertised in the newspaper looking for us. He mentioned me-- he particularly mentioned that I had a birthmark on my leg, and so he mentioned that. And in time, he did find us and joined us.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, at that point, my father and my grandfather and all the men had to think of a way of earning a living. And they decided to take dairy products from the country, bring it to the city, and that's how they earned some money. We all lived together in one big-- it wasn't so big, but one ar-- one house. And after a while, my father and my mother and I and my sister left them, because we were just too crowded. And we went to a town called Winniki. At that point, my father got a job working in a coke-- like a peat factory, you know, for coal. And so that's what he was doing. And I-- I remember my mother got very sick one time. And once again, there was very little medical treatment. She-- I remember this, because it was so frightening. She had leeches put behind her ear and they sucked out the blood. I guess she must have had infection or fever. And she got better.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in this town Winnik--

speaker: interviewee
text: Winniki.

speaker: interviewer
text: Winniki.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it nearby to the rest of your family? Were you in contact with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't recall being in contact with them. I don't remember-- I don't remember that at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your-- do you recall your sense about where you were or what was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: I recall that there was something terribly wrong. No one sat me down and explained anything. But somehow, I knew-- I just-- I always felt, as a child during the war, I knew what was going on, without having really been told. Because I heard the adults talking. But I knew there was something bad happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you there for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Months, maybe-- months. And my-- that's when my sister got-- my baby sister got sick with diphtheria and she died.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember her being sick. I don't remember the burial or the death, or I don't remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when do you think that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that must have been in 1940, or close to '41-- something like that. '40, '41.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop now and change tapes.
